title: Minimum time take to burn a BT from a given node

content.problemStatement:
You are given the **root** of a binary tree and a **target node value**. Suppose the target node catches fire at time `t = 0`.

In each **1 second**, the fire spreads from any burning node to all **directly connected nodes** — that is, to its **left child**, **right child**, and **parent**.

Your task is to determine the **minimum time required** to completely burn the entire binary tree.

Rules of Fire Spread:
Fire spreads simultaneously to **all connected nodes** every second.
The tree is **connected** (no isolated nodes).
All node values are **unique**.

Input Format


The first line contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.




Each test case consists of **two lines of input**:




The first line contains two space-separated integers:



NNN — the number of nodes in the binary tree
XXX — the value of the target node from which the fire starts



The second line contains NNN space-separated values representing the **level-order traversal** of the tree.
Use **`-1`** (or `null`) to denote missing children.







Output Format

For each test case, output on a new line a single integer —
the **minimum time required to burn the entire binary tree** starting from node XXX.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer T, denoting the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines of input: The first line contains two space-separated integers: N — the number of nodes in the binary tree and X — the value of the target node from which the fire starts. The second line contains N space-separated values representing the level-order traversal of the tree. Use -1 to denote missing children.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line a single integer — the minimum time required to burn the entire binary tree starting from node X.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: At t = 0: Node 10 is on fire. At t = 1: Nodes 5 and 15 catch fire. At t = 2: Nodes 2 and 7 catch fire. At t = 3: Node 20 catches fire.
Explanation: Fire Spread: t = 0: Node 4 burns. t = 1: Node 5 burns. t = 2: Nodes 3 and 6 burn. t = 3: Nodes 1 and 7 burn. t = 4: Nodes 9 burn. t = 5: Nodes 8 burn.
Explanation: Fire Spread: t = 0: Node 3 burns. t = 1: Nodes 1 and 5 burn. t = 2: Node 2 burns. t = 3: Node 4 burns. t = 4: Node 6 burns.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a BFS traversal of the tree. We start by finding the target node and then perform a BFS traversal from that node. We keep track of the time it takes to burn each node and return the maximum time as the result.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(T * N) total. The BFS traversal takes O(N) time in the worst case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — the space complexity is dominated by the queue used for BFS traversal.
